description:
You are the performance engineer on a micro-services team.  
    Before you start profiling the network layer of the application, you must make sure every
    internal hostname resolves to the IP address the developers expect.

    Two helper files have already been placed on the machine for you:

        1. /home/user/profiling/host_targets.txt  
           • One hostname per line.  
           • Order matters – do **NOT** sort or reorder the names.

        2. /home/user/profiling/ip_map.txt  
           • A space-separated list that mimics /etc/hosts:   
             first column = IPv4 address, second column = canonical hostname.  
           • No extra columns, tabs or comments.

    Your job:

        1. For every hostname that appears (in the same order) inside
           /home/user/profiling/host_targets.txt,
           “resolve” it against /home/user/profiling/ip_map.txt and extract the matching IP
           address.  You may use any standard command-line tooling (grep, awk, cut, join,
           etc.); no external programming languages are required.

        2. Create a new comma-separated log file called
           /home/user/profiling/dns_profile.log.  
           The file **must contain exactly three lines** (one line per
           hostname) and follow **this exact byte-for-byte format**:

              hostname,ip_address\n

           Concretely, the finished file must look exactly like:

              frontend.local,192.168.50.10
              backend.local,192.168.50.20
              db.local,192.168.50.30

           Notes:  
           • There are **no leading or trailing spaces** on any line.  
           • Lines are separated by a single Unix newline (`\n`).  
           • The file ends with a final newline.

        3. After writing the file, print its contents to the terminal so your
           colleagues (and the automated grader) can see the result.

    Only /home/user/profiling/dns_profile.log will be graded, and it will be compared
    byte-for-byte with the expected output above.  Extra blank lines, spaces or columns will
    cause the check to fail, so pay close attention to formatting.

    Hint (no need to follow verbatim): a typical solution can be achieved with
    three or four commands that read host_targets.txt, look each name up in
    ip_map.txt and redirect the properly formatted output into dns_profile.log.
truth:
The grader will verify that the file /home/user/profiling/dns_profile.log **exists**
    and contains exactly the following three lines and nothing else:

        frontend.local,192.168.50.10\n
        backend.local,192.168.50.20\n
        db.local,192.168.50.30\n

    Initial files present before the agent starts:

      1. /home/user/profiling/host_targets.txt
           ┌────────────────────────┐
           │frontend.local         │
           │backend.local          │
           │db.local               │
           └────────────────────────┘

      2. /home/user/profiling/ip_map.txt
           ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
           │192.168.50.10 frontend.local       │
           │192.168.50.20 backend.local        │
           │192.168.50.30 db.local             │
           └────────────────────────────────────┘

    No other special files or environment variables are required.
